category_title: Students with high sensory interests and emerging joint attention
minimal_context: The student and teacher are in the school dinner hall playing with a dog and a ball to sustain interaction.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Here is the dog and the ball. Let's play together.

STUDENT: (Looks at the dog and throws the ball, showing an expression of pleasure.)

TUTOR: The dog brought the ball back. Throw it again.

STUDENT: (Takes the ball but just holds it, looking at the dog without throwing it.)

TUTOR: It is time to keep playing with the dog.

STUDENT: (Suddenly drops the ball, turns completely away from the dog, and begins engaging in repetitive sensory behaviors, switching off from the interaction.)

options.A: Use the dog-student interaction as a 'way in' to engage with the student—for example, by commenting on and praising interactions, showing interest, remaining close by, looking, and smiling.
options.B: Make regulative practices explicit during the session with the dog so the student knows exactly what they need to do to demonstrate competency and compliance.
options.C: Provide opportunities to respond (OTR) at a level of four to six responses per minute throughout the 20-minute session with the dog.
options.D: Pre-plan specific teacher responses to potential non-compliance, ensuring there is a predetermined reaction ready to implement during the 20-minute session with the dog.
